Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic most of the time. I'd try to block out time in the evenings after my part-time job, but things rarely went according to plan. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend a lot of time making diagrams and color-coded notes from the lecture materials. With 20 online courses to juggle, I basically had to prioritize whatever was due soonest. Some weeks I'd study 13 hours straight across a few days, other weeks it was scattered whenever I could grab a moment.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines—I wish I could say I was more consistent. When multiple assignments piled up, I'd go into overdrive and study at really odd hours, sometimes late at night when my anxiety was keeping me awake anyway. I tried to set regular times on Sundays to review the week ahead, but competing responsibilities like work and extracurriculars constantly disrupted that. Deadlines basically dictated my entire schedule, which was exhausting but somehow I made it work.
Q3: Usually what prompted me to log in was either a deadline notification or this nagging anxiety that I was missing something important. I'd also log in when I had a few minutes between things just to check if new materials were posted. Sometimes I'd see a discussion post notification and feel obligated to contribute something. The fear of falling behind was honestly my biggest motivator—not the healthiest reason, but it kept me logging in frequently.
Q4: Yeah, after the first few weeks, checking the LMS became almost automatic, like checking my phone for messages. I'd wake up and log in before even getting out of bed just to see what was happening. It became routine because I was enrolled in so many courses that I couldn't afford to miss updates. The anxiety of potentially missing a deadline made it a habit, though I'm not sure that's the kind of routine anyone should aspire to.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me—I watched over 137 minutes worth because seeing concepts visually really helps them stick in my brain. I also found the discussion forums surprisingly useful when other students explained things in different ways than the instructor. Any resource that had diagrams, flowcharts, or visual summaries was gold for me. The peer interactions helped me feel less alone in struggling with difficult material.
Q6: I'll admit I often skipped over long text-based readings or PDFs that were just walls of text—my brain just doesn't absorb information that way. Optional supplementary materials usually got pushed to the bottom of my priority list because I was barely keeping up with required stuff. Some of the older forum threads I'd ignore because scrolling through them felt overwhelming when I was already stressed. If something didn't seem directly connected to an assignment or exam, it often got delayed indefinitely.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could clearly see how it connected to an assignment or exam—that direct relevance was crucial. Visual content that I could screenshot or turn into notes felt valuable because I knew I'd use it later. Discussion posts felt worthwhile when they actually clarified something I was confused about rather than just repeating the lectures. Honestly, anything that reduced my anxiety about upcoming deadlines felt like time well spent.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't really afford most of the time. Required activities came with this pressure and urgency that optional ones just didn't have. Sometimes I'd look at optional materials and feel guilty for not doing them, which just added to my stress without actually helping me learn. When I did engage with optional content, it was usually during rare moments when I felt caught up—which wasn't often.
Q9: Absolutely—I did tons of studying that never showed up in the LMS. I'd download lecture slides and videos when I had brief internet access, then study them offline for hours since I don't have reliable internet at home. I made physical flashcards and filled notebooks with visual diagrams and mind maps. I also used free YouTube videos and other external resources to supplement what I was learning, especially when the course materials didn't explain things visually enough.
Q10: There were many times I'd be studying intensely from printed notes or downloaded materials at home where there's no internet. I'd spend hours redrawing diagrams and creating visual summaries that helped me understand complex concepts. Group study sessions with classmates happened in person, not through the LMS. My assignment scores don't fully reflect how much time I actually spent wrestling with the material offline.
Q11: All the time—this was basically my standard approach. I'd log in, download everything I needed for the week, and then work through it offline over several days. My internet situation forced me to be strategic about when and how I accessed online materials. I'd watch a video once on campus, take detailed notes, and then review those notes repeatedly at home. The LMS probably showed one view, but I'd engage with that content five or six times offline.
Q12: There were definitely moments when everything felt like too much, especially around midterms when multiple courses had deadlines clustering together. My anxiety would spike and I'd feel paralyzed, unable to decide what to tackle first. Financial stress from my low resources situation sometimes made it hard to focus on academics. The mindfulness intervention helped a bit, but there were still days when opening the LMS felt overwhelming rather than productive.
Q13: Workload was probably the biggest factor—with 20 courses, I was constantly triaging what needed attention most urgently. Fatigue hit hard because I was also working and doing extracurriculars, so sometimes I'd log in but not really absorb anything. Confusion about course expectations sometimes made me avoid certain modules until I absolutely had to face them. When competing responsibilities piled up, my LMS usage became very strategic and survival-focused rather than thorough.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd have videos playing while my mind was somewhere else entirely, worrying about other deadlines or life stuff. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really processing them, just trying to feel like I was doing something productive. Sometimes I'd log in and click around aimlessly because I was too mentally exhausted to actually engage. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than genuine learning.
Q15: I definitely postponed discussion posts and peer interactions even though I knew they were valuable for my learning. The anxiety of putting my thoughts out there sometimes felt more overwhelming than the benefit of participating. I'd also avoid starting large assignments until closer to deadlines because the scope felt paralyzing. It's frustrating because I knew these behaviors weren't helping me, but the avoidance felt like self-protection in the moment.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my entire LMS usage pattern—I'd become hyperactive in the days before something was due. I'd log in multiple times a day as deadlines approached, checking requirements and looking for any hints or clarifications. My engagement was very cyclical, with peaks around due dates and valleys in between. It wasn't ideal, but with so many courses, deadline-driven studying was the only way I could manage.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected my engagement, though sometimes in complicated ways. Positive feedback motivated me to engage more with similar activities. Critical feedback sometimes triggered my anxiety and made me hesitant to participate in discussions for a while. I appreciated detailed feedback most because it helped me understand what to focus on, even if it was initially hard to receive.
Q18: The course structure had a huge impact on how I participated. Clear weekly modules with explicit deadlines helped me plan my limited study time more effectively. When the structure was unclear or materials were scattered, my anxiety would increase and I'd spend more time figuring out what to do than actually learning. I appreciated courses that front-loaded important information so I could download and plan offline.
Q19: Visual materials and clearly organized modules made engagement much easier for me. Having consistent deadline patterns helped me manage my schedule across all 20 courses. What made engagement harder was when important information was buried in long text documents or when expectations weren't clearly stated upfront. The lack of reliable internet access at home meant any design that required constant online presence was challenging for me.
Q20: I can totally relate to this pattern. Sometimes you're frantically searching through materials trying to understand something, clicking around looking for answers, but the actual assignment just isn't coming together. High anxiety can drive you to check the LMS constantly without being able to focus enough to produce quality work. Also, if someone is struggling with the content, they might be engaging heavily with materials but still not feel confident enough to submit work.
Q21: That definitely reflects part of my experience—my attendance shows as really low at 1.5%, but I still managed a Merit grade. A lot of my actual learning happened offline with downloaded materials and personal notes. Some students are just efficient at extracting what they need from brief LMS visits. The data doesn't capture the hours spent studying from printed materials or the learning that happens in study groups.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show login patterns, what materials were accessed, and submission times—the quantitative stuff. What they miss is the quality of engagement, the emotional state while studying, and all the offline learning that happens. They can't capture whether someone was genuinely focused or just anxiously clicking around. For someone like me with limited internet, the logs massively underrepresent my actual study time and effort.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material until it makes sense, not just going through the motions. It's about feeling curious and connected to what you're learning, even when it's challenging. For me, real engagement involves creating visual representations, discussing ideas with peers, and being able to apply concepts. It's both cognitive and emotional—you have to be mentally present and invested in understanding.
Q24: The LMS data would never show how much anxiety I carried throughout this course, or how the mindfulness intervention helped me cope even if my mood stayed low. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent studying from downloaded materials at home without internet, or the physical notebooks full of diagrams I created. My extracurricular commitments and financial stress that competed for my attention are invisible in the data. Most importantly, it can't show how hard I was trying despite feeling overwhelmed most of the time.
